entries.title: Allegories of Hiram Abiff
entries.short_summary: This introductory section outlines the scope of the work "Allegories of Hiram Abiff," which delves into exoteric and esoteric philosophies drawn from Christianity, Rosicrucianism, and ancient Egyptian mystery schools. It emphasizes Freemasonry's traditional method of conveying moral and ethical truths through allegory and symbols, asserting that initiates must engage with both their intellect and their emotions to fully comprehend these teachings.
entries.full_summary:
This introductory section outlines the scope of the work "Allegories of Hiram Abiff," which delves into exoteric and esoteric philosophies drawn from Christianity, Rosicrucianism, and ancient Egyptian mystery schools. It emphasizes Freemasonry's traditional method of conveying moral and ethical truths through allegory and symbols, asserting that initiates must engage with both their intellect and their emotions to fully comprehend these teachings.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Symbolism of the Master Mason Degree

The Master Mason Degree initiates the candidate in darkness, signifying a state of equilibrium and preparedness for sacred ground. The candidate commits fully to the Fraternity, placing faith in the Three Great Lights, and is introduced to working tools, with one symbolizing Brotherly love. The central Mystery Drama involves the candidate assuming the role of Grand Master Hiram Abiff, forging a link to the Fraternity's inner soul and ancient Mystery Schools. This experience aims for self-realization, and the symbolism itself is ancient, evolving culturally over time.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0003 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Low Twelve

This section explains the ancient symbolism of 'Low Twelve,' contrasting it with 'High Twelve.' It details how the number twelve signifies completion, fulfillment, and human life, drawing connections to the twelve signs of the Zodiac and the twelve edges of a cube. Low Twelve specifically represents midnight, the darkest time of the night, while High Twelve denotes noon.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0007 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Section

This section describes the Third Section of a degree, focusing on hieroglyphical emblems, moral lessons, and extensive details regarding the construction of King Solomon's Temple. It covers the temple's foundation, location on Mount Moriah, construction duration, the absence of tool sounds during building, and the vast workforce involved, including Grand Masters, Masters, Fellow Crafts, and Entered Apprentices. The section also elaborates on the two celebrated pillars placed at the temple's entrance, explaining their symbolic significance as a memorial to the Israelites' deliverance from Egyptian bondage, referencing the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0074 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section outlines the significant duties, responsibilities, and moral obligations of a newly raised Master Mason. It emphasizes maintaining the fraternity's reputation, guiding less informed brethren, upholding ancient landmarks, and exemplifying virtues like morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, all while remaining faithful to one's trust and vows, mirroring the example of Hiram Abiff.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0093 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Third Circuit

This section, titled "Third Circuit," details a solemn ritualistic exchange among lodge officers and the Chaplain, reflecting on the transient nature of life, the inevitability of death, and the sorrows of mortality. It progresses through lamentations of human frailty and grief, balanced by expressions of faith in God's justice, mercy, and the promise of eternal life and resurrection. The ritual includes prayers for strength, wisdom, and comfort for the bereaved, culminating in a collective affirmation of hope and trust in divine beneficence, often associated with a Lodge of Sorrow.

This section describes a solemn Masonic ceremony, likely a Lodge of Sorrow, where the Master reflects on the inevitability of death and the transient nature of life. The Chaplain reads from 1 Corinthians 15, discussing the resurrection of the dead and the transformation from corruptible to incorruptible bodies. A choir sings from Job 19 about seeing God after death. The Orator is then called upon to recount the virtues of the deceased, with an emphasis on Masonic charity and non-judgment.

This section details the 'Third Circuit' of a Masonic memorial service, which includes scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job, a choir performance, and an oration emphasizing truth and Masonic Charity towards the deceased. The ritual proceeds with the Junior Warden sprinkling corn, the Senior Warden sprinkling wine, and the Master sprinkling perfumed oil on the coffin, each accompanied by symbolic interpretations related to resurrection, spiritual refreshment, and consolation. The ceremony concludes with the Master placing a lambskin apron and holding a white glove on the coffin, symbolizing innocence, purity, and the hope of a welcome reception in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

This section details the ritualistic farewell for a deceased Master Mason, highlighting the symbolism of the white glove as an emblem of Fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, and the sprig of evergreen as an emblem of Faith in the immortality of the soul. It describes the Master's address, the placement of the evergreen on the coffin, a collective farewell by the brethren, and a three-circuit procession around the coffin accompanied by Grand Honors.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0111 -->

entries.symbolic_meaning:
Freemasonry utilizes allegory and symbols as the most effective means to teach moral and ethical truths, suggesting that these methods convey deeper meanings than literal instruction alone.

The entrance in darkness signifies a new, unseen light and a state of equilibrium, preparing the candidate for sacred ground. The exalted working tool symbolizes the spreading of Brotherly love. Participating in the Mystery Drama and assuming Hiram Abiff's role forges a link with the Fraternity's inner soul and archetypal forces, connecting to ancient Mystery Schools. The experience in the 'Holy of Holies' leads to self-realization, where one may momentarily forget their former self and realize their true identity.

The number twelve, in ancient symbolism, denotes completion, derived from the twelve signs of the Zodiac forming a complete circle and the twelve edges of a cube symbolizing the earth. It also signifies the fulfillment of a deed and serves as an emblem of human life. High Twelve corresponds to noon, when the sun is at its zenith, while Low Twelve denotes midnight, representing the blackest time of the night.

The section illustrates hieroglyphical emblems and inculcates moral lessons. The two celebrated pillars at the temple entrance symbolize the happy deliverance of the Israelites from Egyptian bondage and commemorate the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud, which provided light and darkness respectively during the Exodus. Their placement ensured the event was continually before the eyes of those going to and from divine worship.

The instruction to "imitate the example of that celebrated artist whom you have this evening represented" directly refers to Hiram Abiff, symbolizing fidelity, integrity, and steadfastness in the face of adversity, even unto death. The Master Mason is expected to embody these virtues in their conduct and adherence to Masonic principles.

The "Third Circuit" symbolizes the journey of life, marked by inevitable suffering, loss, and the ultimate certainty of death. The lamentations represent the human experience of grief and the transient nature of earthly existence ("life is a vapor," "days are passed"). The responses from the Chaplain, particularly the references to a "tree planted by the waters" and "a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens," symbolize enduring faith, divine sustenance, and the promise of immortality and resurrection beyond the grave. The ritual as a whole serves as a memento mori, reminding participants of mortality while simultaneously offering comfort and hope through spiritual belief and the prospect of an afterlife.

Life is presented as 'death’s vestibule' and a 'journey to the grave,' symbolizing the transient nature of earthly existence. The comparison of human life to flowers and grass emphasizes fragility and impermanence. The 'valley of the shadow of death' symbolizes the journey through mortality with divine comfort. The scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the hope of resurrection, transformation from a natural to a spiritual body, and victory over death. The seed analogy in 1 Corinthians 15 symbolizes the transformation of the body after death into a new, spiritual form. The 'broad mantle of charity' symbolizes Masonic non-judgment and compassion towards the deceased.

The scriptural readings from 1 Corinthians and Job symbolize resurrection, incorruption, transformation from mortal to immortal, and the belief in a Redeemer. The corn of nourishment symbolizes the resurrection of the body, sown in corruption and raised in incorruption, glory, power, and as a spiritual body. The wine of refreshment symbolizes spiritual refreshment, divine grace, blessings, and comfort for those afflicted or distressed. The oil of joy symbolizes comfort, consolation for the bereaved, healing of heart-wounds, the virtue of charity, increased affection, leniency, indulgence, forgiveness, and a joyful passage through death. The lambskin apron, an emblem of innocence and a Mason's badge, denotes the hope that the departed brother's soul, purified from imperfections, has found a welcome in the Supreme Grand Lodge above.

The white glove is an emblem of Fidelity, symbolizing Masonic friendship and brotherly love, and signifies an increased obligation to the living brethren. The sprig of evergreen is an emblem of Masonic Faith in the immortality of the soul, representing the eternal principle in man that survives death and blooms perpetually. It denotes trust that the deceased's immortal spirit has entered a celestial temple and found acceptance with the Supreme Grand Master.
entries.candidate_lesson:
An initiate in Freemasonry must approach its teachings not merely with intellectual understanding but also with emotional and spiritual engagement, integrating both mind and heart to truly grasp the moral and ethical truths presented.

The candidate learns to be fully committed to the Fraternity and to place complete faith in the Three Great Lights. They are taught the importance of Brotherly love, symbolized by a specific working tool. Through the Mystery Drama and assuming the role of Hiram Abiff, the candidate is meant to connect with the inner soul of the Fraternity and ancient traditions, leading to a profound self-realization.

The symbolism of 'Low Twelve' and the number twelve teaches about the cycles of life, the concept of completion and fulfillment in one's journey, and the inevitable passage of time, including the darkest hours, which can be interpreted as a reflection on mortality.

The section emphasizes the importance of peace and good fellowship among workmen, as exemplified by King Solomon's wise organization of the temple builders, which prevented envy, discord, or confusion. It also highlights the significance of divine guidance and deliverance, as symbolized by the temple's pillars, reminding candidates of historical and spiritual liberation.

As a Master Mason, one is bound by duty, honor, and gratitude to be faithful to their trust, uphold the dignity of their character, and enforce obedience to the Institution's tenets. This includes correcting errors of less informed brethren, preserving the fraternity's reputation through morality, harmony, equality, and universal benevolence, and diligently guarding the ancient landmarks. The Master Mason must remain true to their vows and imitate the fidelity of Hiram Abiff.

The candidate is taught to confront the reality of human mortality and the sorrows of life with patience, faith, and resignation to divine will. It emphasizes the importance of trust in God ("He who trusteth in the Lord"), the pursuit of good deeds, and the cultivation of a good conscience. The lesson instills hope in a glorious hereafter and the belief in an eternal spiritual existence, encouraging brethren to draw healing and profit from sorrow and to live a life of charity and faith, preparing for a peaceful transition at the end of life's journey.

Candidates are taught to contemplate their own mortality and the inevitability of death. They learn the importance of living a virtuous life, as one's deeds will be remembered. The section instills hope in resurrection and spiritual transformation beyond physical death, and emphasizes the Masonic principle of charity and non-judgment towards deceased brethren.

Brethren are taught the importance of truth and Masonic Charity, particularly when speaking of the deceased, emphasizing non-judgment and extending compassion. The ritual reinforces the belief in resurrection and immortality, encouraging contemplation of mortality and the afterlife. It also instills lessons on the virtues of charity, friendship, kindness, brotherhood, and honor, and the importance of leniency, indulgence, and forgiveness towards others. The ceremony provides comfort and consolation through faith and brotherhood during times of loss, fostering an abiding trust in divine mercy for a joyful passage beyond death.

The section reinforces the Masonic principles of fidelity, friendship, and brotherly love, emphasizing that these obligations extend to the living even after a brother's passing. It also instills faith in the immortality of the soul and the hope of a reunion in a celestial realm, providing comfort and a spiritual perspective on death.
tradition_notes: Freemasonry traditionally teaches moral and ethical truths by veiling them in allegory and illustrating them with symbols, considering this the most assured method.
The engagement of both the brain/mind and the heart is a traditional expectation for initiates to fully comprehend Masonic teachings.
The legend of the Master Mason Degree is consistent with ancient Mystery Schools, suggesting a communion with archetypal forces that are the foundation of the tradition.

The symbolism encountered in this Degree can be traced back for hundreds, if not thousands, of years, with some elements being almost identical to ancient usage, while others have taken on cultural flavor from successive conveyors.
The symbolism discussed is rooted in ancient traditions, referencing the Zodiac and the cube as sources for the meaning of the number twelve.
Josephus is cited as an informant regarding the temple's construction duration and the absence of rain.
Sacred history is referenced for the detail that no tool sounds were heard during the temple's building.
The placement of the two pillars is presented as a tradition to memorialize the deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the pillars of fire and cloud.
The text refers to "ancient landmarks of the Institution," emphasizing the importance of preserving long-standing Masonic principles and traditions.
It also mentions "established usages and customs of the fraternity," highlighting adherence to traditional practices.
The ritual involves a structured dialogue among lodge officers and the Chaplain.
The repetition of "honors" is a specific ritualistic action.
The chanting of anthems and prayers is a traditional part of Masonic ceremonies, particularly those of a solemn nature.
The explicit mention of "LODGE OF SORROW" indicates this ritual is part of or related to a specific Masonic memorial service.
The kneeling of brethren during prayer is a common practice.
The response "So mote it be" is a traditional Masonic affirmation.
The text describes a ritualistic sequence involving the Master, Chaplain, Choir, and Orator, characteristic of a Masonic memorial service or Lodge of Sorrow.
The use of specific scripture passages (1 Corinthians 15:35-57 and Job 19:25-27) is a traditional element in Masonic ceremonies concerning mortality and resurrection.
The ritual described is part of a Masonic memorial service, implicitly a 'Lodge of Sorrow'.
The tradition of an Orator recounting the virtues and good deeds of the deceased is observed.
The symbolic offerings of corn, wine, and oil by the Junior Warden, Senior Warden, and Master, respectively, are a traditional part of the service.
The lambskin apron is traditionally recognized as an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The fraternity of Free and Accepted Masons is traditionally understood to have been instituted by Ancient Grand Master Solomon, King of Israel, who taught that Truth is a divine attribute.

The ritual includes specific actions like holding and laying the sprig of evergreen, a collective farewell, and a three-circuit procession with Grand Honors, indicating established Masonic funeral practices.
caution_notes: Do not swerve from your duty.
Do not violate your vows.
Do not betray your trust.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, and teaches us to throw around their foibles, whatever they may have been, the broad mantle of charity.

Masonry does not sit in judgment upon her dead, teaching brethren to extend the broad mantle of Masonic Charity over their foibles, allowing human nature's apologies to plead for those who can no longer plead for themselves.

The Orator is charged to speak naught but the simple truth; if no good can be truthfully spoken of a brother, Masonry seals the lips, as Truth is a divine attribute and the foundation of every virtue.

entries.title: Who Was Hiram Abiff?
entries.short_summary: This section introduces Hiram Abiff, the architect of King Solomon's Temple, and recounts the core narrative of the Hiramic Legend. It details his role as one of the first three Grand Masters, his refusal to divulge the Master's Word to three Fellowcraft Masons, his subsequent murder with Masonic tools, the burial of his body marked by a sprig of Acacia, and his eventual discovery and the institution of substitute words and gestures by King Solomon. The text also explores the scriptural basis for the name Hiram Abiff, noting the presence of 'Hiram' or 'Huram' in Chronicles and Kings, and the potential etymological link of 'Abiff' to the Hebrew word for 'father' or 'fatherless' (widow's son).
entries.full_summary:
This section introduces Hiram Abiff, the architect of King Solomon's Temple, and recounts the core narrative of the Hiramic Legend. It details his role as one of the first three Grand Masters, his refusal to divulge the Master's Word to three Fellowcraft Masons, his subsequent murder with Masonic tools, the burial of his body marked by a sprig of Acacia, and his eventual discovery and the institution of substitute words and gestures by King Solomon. The text also explores the scriptural basis for the name Hiram Abiff, noting the presence of 'Hiram' or 'Huram' in Chronicles and Kings, and the potential etymological link of 'Abiff' to the Hebrew word for 'father' or 'fatherless' (widow's son).

### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Three Ruffians

This section explains the symbolic significance of the Three Ruffians in Masonic ritual. Their attempt to unlawfully obtain secrets and the resulting dire consequences symbolize the pursuit of Divine Truth through unearned or illicit means, equating such actions to theft and murder. The narrative also presents the Ruffians as allegories for internal enemies such as ignorance, passions, and negative attitudes, which Masons are taught to control and subdue.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0006 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0008 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Lion of the Tribe of Judah

This section explores the symbolism of the lion, particularly as the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'. It details its historical association with might, royalty, the Hebrew Tribe of Judah, and King Solomon. The text also delves into its medieval symbolism of resurrection, referencing tales of lion cubs being brought to life by their father's breath and roaring lions representing the resurrection on the Last Day. The lion's majestic nature and association with the sun are also noted.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0008 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0012 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Sprig of Acacia

The sprig of acacia holds significant symbolic meaning, particularly among Hebrew people, who traditionally planted it at graves to mark the location and signify a belief in immortality. Its evergreen nature, hardness, and durability led to its association with immortality and innocence. Ancient Jews and Egyptians shared this belief, with Egyptians also linking it to rebirth. Tradition suggests acacia was used in the construction of the Temple's furniture and tabernacle, and in the Near East, it is believed to have formed the Crown of Thorns. The plant itself is thorny, abounds in the Middle East, and has red and white flowers.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0012 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Regularity and Recognition in Freemasonry

This section defines and differentiates between 'Regularity' and 'Recognition' in Freemasonry. Regularity is a subjective standard for Grand Lodges, often based on Anderson's Constitutions of 1723, which outlines rules like candidate acceptance, display of the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass, and the integral role of the Hiramic Legend in the Third Degree. Recognition, in contrast, is an objective state of amity between sovereign Grand Lodges, allowing members to visit and hold dual membership. The text also defines a 'Clandestine Lodge' as one operating without a legitimate charter.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->

This section defines the three Ornaments of a Lodge: the Mosaic Pavement, the Indented Tessel, and the Blazing Star. It describes their physical representation and their symbolic meanings within Freemasonry.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Our Ancient Brethren

This section details the historical and contemporary dedications of Masonic Lodges, noting that ancient brethren dedicated their lodges to King Solomon, while modern lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist. It describes the significant emblem of a point within a circle, bordered by two perpendicular parallel lines representing the Saints John, with the Holy Scriptures resting on top. The symbolism of each component is explained: the point signifies an individual brother, the circle represents a moral boundary against passions and prejudices, and the parallel lines symbolize moral rectitude and the summer and winter solstices. The Holy Scriptures denote divine origin and inspiration. The text also states that lodges are traditionally situated East and West, mirroring King Solomon's Temple, and concludes by listing the principal tenets of Masonry: Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0033 -->

Source section: Brother A. B.

This section congratulates a Brother on being passed to the Second Degree, emphasizing that Freemasonry values internal qualifications over external ones. It highlights the institution's resilience, having survived the destruction of Solomon's magnificent Temple. The text explains that Masonic mysteries are transmitted through attentive listening and faithful preservation, using architectural tools and symbolic emblems to impart wise truths across generations. It concludes by reminding all Masons, from the youngest Entered Apprentice to the Worshipful Master, to humbly bow before the sacred name of Deity.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Ceremonies at the Grave

This section details the Masonic funeral ceremony conducted at the grave site. It outlines the positioning of officers and brethren, the attire worn, and the sequence of the service, which includes a prayer offered by the Master, Chaplain, or a designated Brother, followed by a solemn exhortation from the Master. The ceremony emphasizes the contemplation of mortality, the uncertainty of human life, and the importance of preparing for eternity through faith, hope, and charity.

This section details the Masonic funeral ceremony conducted at the grave, including the symbolic deposit of the lamb-skin apron and evergreen. It describes the ritual of the Funeral Grand Honors, complete with specific gestures and spoken words, and concludes with prayers and blessings offered by the Master or Chaplain, emphasizing themes of mortality, immortality, and divine mercy.

Planting a sprig of acacia at the head of a grave to mark its location.
Members of recognized Grand Lodges are permitted to visit one another's lodges.

Only Brethren from recognized Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
The Mosaic Pavement is a representation of the ground floor of King Solomon’s Temple.
The Indented Tessel is the beautiful border that surrounds the Mosaic Pavement.
The Blazing Star is located in the center of the Indented Tessel.
Lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist.
Every regular and well-governed Lodge represents a point within a circle, embordered by two perpendicular parallel lines, with the Holy Scriptures resting on top.
Lodges should be situated due East and West.
The attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue.
Mysteries of Freemasonry are safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts.
All Masons, from Entered Apprentice to Worshipful Master, should humbly bow with reverence to Deity.
The temple was founded in the fourth year of Solomon's reign, on the second day of the month Zif.
It was located on Mount Moriah, near where Abraham was to offer Isaac and where David appeased the destroying angel.
Construction took over seven years, during which it reportedly did not rain in the daytime.
No sound of axe, hammer, or iron tool was heard during construction.
The temple was supported by 1453 pillars and 2906 pilasters, all of Parian marble.
The workforce included 3 Grand Masters, 3300 Masters/Overseers, 80000 Fellow Crafts, and 70000 Entered Apprentices.
Two celebrated pillars were placed in front of the temple's porch, one on the left and one on the right.
The funeral director places the casket.
The minister concludes their services.
The Master, hat removed, takes position at the head of the grave.
The Chaplain is on the Master's right.
The Senior Warden is at the foot of the grave.
The Junior Warden is at the center of the grave, to the left of the Master.
Officers wear their jewels.
Brethren gather behind the Master or in a convenient place, wearing Aprons.
The Lodge service begins with a prayer.
The prayer is offered by the Master, Chaplain, or a designated Brother.
All respond with 'So mote it be' after the prayer.
The Master delivers an exhortation.
The Master presents and deposits the lamb-skin or White Apron upon the coffin.
The Master holds and deposits the evergreen in the grave.
Only the Three Principal officers conducting the service place the sprig of Acacia upon the coffin, each saying, "Farewell my Brother."
All Masons present join in giving the Funeral Grand Honors.

Funeral Grand Honors involve crossing both arms on the breast (left uppermost, palms touching shoulders), raising them above the head (palms inward, looking upward), and then extending them toward the ground with the head bowed.

During the Funeral Grand Honors, brethren audibly pronounce: "We cherish his memory here" (arms crossed), "We commend his spirit to God, who gave it" (hands above head), and "And consign his body to the earth, whence it came" (hands extended to ground).
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, with each brother required to pronounce the words audibly.
The Chaplain or Senior Warden recites a passage from scripture: "Despise not thou the chastening of the Lord..."
The Master calls for devout prayer to the heavenly Father.
The Master or Chaplain leads a prayer acknowledging the passing of the brother, asking for divine assistance, and seeking comfort for the bereaved.
All Masons respond with "So mote it be" after the prayer and the Master's final blessing.
The Master pronounces a final blessing: "The Lord bless thee, and keep thee..."
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The sprig of Acacia marks the shallow grave of Hiram Abiff, symbolizing remembrance and potentially immortality or rebirth.

The Three Ruffians' attempt to obtain secrets not rightfully theirs symbolizes the endeavor to acquire knowledge of Divine Truth through means other than faithfulness, thereby making the perpetrator a thief and a murderer. They also represent the internal enemies within each individual: ignorance, passions, and attitudes, which Masons are enjoined to control and subdue.

The lion symbolizes might, royalty, and the Tribe of Judah. It is a title for King Solomon. In the Middle Ages, it symbolized resurrection, specifically through tales of lion cubs being revived by their father's breath, and roaring lions representing the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day. The lion is also considered the 'king' of beasts and is associated with the sun due to its mane.

The sprig of acacia symbolizes immortality due to its evergreen nature, hardness, and durability. It also represents innocence because of its evergreen quality. In ancient Egypt, it specifically symbolized rebirth.

The Mosaic Pavement symbolizes human life, which is characterized by a mixture of good and evil. The Indented Tessel, the border surrounding the pavement, symbolizes the numerous blessings and comforts that surround individuals. The Blazing Star, positioned in the center, hieroglyphically represents Divine Providence, upon which Masons rely to obtain these blessings.

The point within the circle represents an individual brother. The circle symbolizes the boundary line beyond which a Mason should never allow his passions or prejudices to betray him. The two perpendicular parallel lines represent St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, and also the summer and winter solstices, which symbolize the lines of moral rectitude. The Holy Scriptures resting upon the top indicate their divine origin and inspiration, signifying that they are supported by divinity itself.

The survival of Freemasonry despite the destruction of Solomon's Temple symbolizes the enduring nature and resilience of the institution, suggesting that its true essence is not in physical structures but in its principles and the hearts of its members. Architectural tools and symbolic emblems are used to represent and imprint wise and serious truths, serving as a visual and conceptual language for moral and philosophical instruction.

The section illustrates hieroglyphical emblems and inculcates moral lessons. The two celebrated pillars at the temple entrance symbolize the happy deliverance of the Israelites from Egyptian bondage and commemorate the miraculous pillars of fire and cloud, which provided light and darkness respectively during the Exodus. Their placement ensured the event was continually before the eyes of those going to and from divine worship.

The ceremony symbolizes the dissolution of the 'earthly tabernacle' and the summoning of the spirit to the 'land where our fathers have gone before us.' It highlights the 'narrow house appointed for all living' as a reminder of the immutable certainty of death and the vanity of human pursuits. The 'cradle and the coffin stand very near to each other' signifies the immediate onset of dying upon birth. The exhortation encourages contemplation of mortality and the hope of eternal life and 'hidden manna' for those who live righteously.

The lamb-skin or White Apron symbolizes innocence, the badge of a Mason, and serves as a reminder of the universal dominion of Death. The mattock, coffin, and grave admonish Masons of their mortality and the eventual return of their bodies to dust. The evergreen is an emblem of faith in the immortality of the soul, reminding Masons of an immortal part that survives the grave and the hope for eternal spring through God's mercy and the merits of the Lion of the Tribe of Judah, leading to the resurrection of bodies as incorruptible as souls.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The narrative of Hiram Abiff emphasizes fidelity to one's obligations and the importance of preserving sacred knowledge, even unto death. The loss of the Master's Word and its replacement with substitutions highlights the concept of something lost and subsequently found or replaced, a central theme in the Master Mason degree.

The candidate is reminded that rewards, particularly knowledge of Divine Truth, must be earned through faithfulness and diligent effort, rather than being obtained through violence, deceit, or any other devious means. Furthermore, the Ruffians serve as a lesson to recognize and overcome one's own internal adversaries, such as ignorance, uncontrolled passions, and negative attitudes.

The enduring nature of the acacia plant, symbolizing immortality and rebirth, encourages contemplation on the continuity of life and the hope beyond physical existence.

It is important for a Mason to understand the concepts of Regularity and Recognition to ensure proper interaction within the Craft and to avoid associating with Clandestine Lodges. This knowledge ensures that one's Masonic activities are conducted within legitimate and recognized jurisdictions.

Candidates learn that human life is a blend of good and evil, and that blessings and comfort are attained through faithful reliance on Divine Providence, as visually represented by the ornaments of the lodge.

A Mason must keep himself circumscribed within the precepts of the Holy Scriptures and the moral rectitude symbolized by the parallel lines. By adhering to these principles, it is impossible for him to materially err. He must never suffer his passions or prejudices to betray him beyond the moral boundary represented by the circle. The principal tenets of Brotherly Love, Relief, and Truth are fundamental to the Masonic profession.

The candidate is taught that true worth in Freemasonry comes from internal character and moral qualifications, not outward appearances or worldly status. They learn the importance of diligently receiving and faithfully preserving Masonic knowledge and mysteries. The lesson also instills a profound sense of reverence and humility towards the Deity, a principle that unites all Masons regardless of their degree or station.

The section emphasizes the importance of peace and good fellowship among workmen, as exemplified by King Solomon's wise organization of the temple builders, which prevented envy, discord, or confusion. It also highlights the significance of divine guidance and deliverance, as symbolized by the temple's pillars, reminding candidates of historical and spiritual liberation.

Brethren are admonished to seriously consider the uncertainty of human life and the certainty of death, and to prepare for eternity. They are urged to maintain the dignified character of their profession, to evince faith through moral conduct, to hold bright hope for future mysteries, and to practice boundless charity. The lesson also includes the importance of faithfully discharging duties to God, neighbor, and self, and extending Masonic charity to the foibles of deceased brethren.

Masons are admonished to remember their mortality and the uncertainty of life, recognizing the transient value of worldly pursuits. The ceremony encourages them to earnestly obey divine laws, avoid dissensions, hatreds, and revenges, and to labor to do good to their fellow-men, aspiring to a spiritual existence beyond this life. It also teaches confidence in God's mercy and provides consolation for those grieving, emphasizing patience and resignation to divine will.
tradition_notes: Freemasons accept Hiram Abiff as the architect of the Temple of Solomon, despite his name not being explicitly found in Scripture.
The name Hiram Abiff is sometimes written as Abif.
The Hebrew word 'ab, meaning 'father' or 'fatherless', is considered a potential etymological root for 'Abiff', linking to the concept of Hiram Abiff as a 'widow's son'.
The lion was the sign of the Tribe of Judah, the royal tribe of the Hebrew Nation. All Kings of Judah were called the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah', including King Solomon.
In the Middle Ages, there were common tales that a lion cub lay dead for three days until its father's breath brought it back to life, symbolizing resurrection.
Representations of roaring lions symbolized the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day.
Hebrew people traditionally planted a sprig of acacia at the head of a grave to mark its location and to show belief in immortality.
Both Jews and Egyptians believed that the acacia, due to its hardness, evergreen nature, and durability, signified immortality.
It is believed that acacia was used to construct most of the furniture and the tabernacle in the Temple.
It is a tradition in the Near East that the Crown of Thorns was made from acacia.
Masonic Law is based in part on Anderson's The Constitutions of the Free-Masons, originally published in 1723.
Anderson's Constitutions listed commonly accepted rules for Grand Lodges, Lodges, and individual members.

Examples of these rules include: acceptance of Candidates irrespective of personal religious beliefs, the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass displayed upon the Altar at all times, the acceptance of men only, and the Hiramic Legend as an integral part of the Third Degree.
The Mosaic Pavement represents the ground floor of King Solomon's Temple.
Ancient Brethren dedicated their Lodges to King Solomon, who was considered their first Most Excellent Grand Master.
Modern Lodges are dedicated to St. John the Baptist and St. John the Evangelist, who are eminent patrons of Masonry.
The emblem of a point within a circle, embordered by two perpendicular parallel lines and topped by the Holy Scriptures, is represented in every regular and well-governed Lodge.
Lodges are traditionally situated due East and West, following the pattern of King Solomon's Temple.
The text references the historical and legendary destruction of Solomon's Temple, a significant event in Masonic tradition, and the subsequent survival of Freemasonry.

It describes the traditional method of transmitting Masonic mysteries verbally ('attentive ear receives the sound from the instructive tongue') and through memory ('safely lodged in the repository of faithful breasts').
Josephus is cited as an informant regarding the temple's construction duration and the absence of rain.
Sacred history is referenced for the detail that no tool sounds were heard during the temple's building.
The placement of the two pillars is presented as a tradition to memorialize the deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the pillars of fire and cloud.
The Master removes his hat at the place of interment.
Officers wear their jewels during the grave service.
Brethren wear Aprons during the grave service.
The lamb-skin or White Apron is an emblem of innocence and the badge of a Mason.
The sprig of Acacia is placed upon the coffin only by the Three Principal officers conducting the service.
The Funeral Grand Honors are a specific ritualistic sequence of gestures and spoken words performed by all Masons present at a funeral service.
The ceremony includes specific prayers and blessings invoking the 'heavenly Father' and the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'.
caution_notes: The dire consequences of the Ruffians' actions serve as a caution against attempting to obtain knowledge or rewards through illicit or unearned means.
A Clandestine Lodge is one that is not working with a legitimate charter from a Grand Lodge and is considered 'in the dark'.
Only Brethren from RECOGNIZED Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
The circle is the boundary line beyond which a Mason is never to suffer his passions or prejudices to betray him.
When the service is used in connection with that of the Knight Templar or the Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite, all preceding parts of the ritual (before the note) should be omitted.
The Lodge should practice the mode of giving the Funeral Grand Honors before leaving its hall, ensuring each brother pronounces the words audibly.
Singing and holding the open Bible by the Chaplain are optional at the Master's discretion.

entries.title: Hiram in Scripture
entries.short_summary: This section details scriptural accounts of Hiram, specifically from 2 Chronicles 2:11-14 and 1 Kings 7:13-14, describing his diverse skills in various materials, his parentage as a widow's son from the tribe of Naphtali with a Tyrian father, and his role in building Solomon's Temple. It clarifies that 'Abif' means 'chief' or 'head' and notes the historical reputation of Tyrians and Sidonians as skilled mechanics and artists. The section also mentions Albert Pike's likely incorrect theory attributing Hiram to a solar deity.

It clarifies that 'Abif' means 'chief' or 'head' and notes the historical reputation of Tyrians and Sidonians as skilled mechanics and artists. entries.title: The Hiramic Legend in Masonic History
entries.short_summary: This section details the historical integration of the Hiramic Legend into Freemasonry, noting its absence in early documents like the Old Charges and Anderson's Constitutions of 1723, and its definitive appearance by 1738. It also draws parallels between the structure of early Masonic degrees and the admission rites of medieval craft guilds, emphasizing the importance of oaths, rules, penalties, and secrecy to protect knowledge and prevent accusations of heresy.
entries.full_summary:
This section details the historical integration of the Hiramic Legend into Freemasonry, noting its absence in early documents like the Old Charges and Anderson's Constitutions of 1723, and its definitive appearance by 1738. It also draws parallels between the structure of early Masonic degrees and the admission rites of medieval craft guilds, emphasizing the importance of oaths, rules, penalties, and secrecy to protect knowledge and prevent accusations of heresy.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0006 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Three Ruffians

This section explains the symbolic significance of the Three Ruffians in Masonic ritual. Their attempt to unlawfully obtain secrets and the resulting dire consequences symbolize the pursuit of Divine Truth through unearned or illicit means, equating such actions to theft and murder. The narrative also presents the Ruffians as allegories for internal enemies such as ignorance, passions, and negative attitudes, which Masons are taught to control and subdue.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0006 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Regularity and Recognition in Freemasonry

This section defines and differentiates between 'Regularity' and 'Recognition' in Freemasonry. Regularity is a subjective standard for Grand Lodges, often based on Anderson's Constitutions of 1723, which outlines rules like candidate acceptance, display of the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass, and the integral role of the Hiramic Legend in the Third Degree. Recognition, in contrast, is an objective state of amity between sovereign Grand Lodges, allowing members to visit and hold dual membership. The text also defines a 'Clandestine Lodge' as one operating without a legitimate charter.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0022 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Prudence

Prudence is defined as the habit of regulating one's life and actions according to reason, enabling wise judgment and determination for present and future happiness. It is a characteristic every Mason should possess, guiding their conduct both within the Lodge and in the wider world. Masons are specifically cautioned to exercise prudence in mixed company to prevent the unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets, signs, tokens, or words.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0039 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0041 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Prudence

Prudence is defined as the habit of wisely judging and prudentially determining actions for present and future happiness, guided by reason. It is a characteristic virtue for every Mason, governing conduct both within the Lodge and in the outside world. Masons are specifically cautioned to exercise prudence in all companies, especially mixed ones, to prevent the unlawful disclosure of Masonic secrets through any sign, token, or word.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0041 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

The Second Section of the ritual recounts the Institution's historical traditions and presents an exemplary instance of virtue, fortitude, and integrity. It emphasizes the importance of spreading brotherly love and affection, which serves as the cement uniting Masons into a sacred society where the only acceptable contention is a noble emulation to excel in work and agreement.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0072 -->

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: V. Organization of Institution

The Masonic institution is fundamentally organized into three Symbolic Degrees. These degrees are characterized by unique modes of recognition, specific ceremonial forms, and notably, the Legend of the Third Degree. All these elements are preserved through the inherent secrecy of the Institution.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The elaborate rites of passage in medieval guilds, and by extension early Masonic degrees, symbolized the trust placed in worthy craftsmen and the protection of esoteric knowledge from outsiders and potential accusations of heresy.

The Three Ruffians' attempt to obtain secrets not rightfully theirs symbolizes the endeavor to acquire knowledge of Divine Truth through means other than faithfulness, thereby making the perpetrator a thief and a murderer. They also represent the internal enemies within each individual: ignorance, passions, and attitudes, which Masons are enjoined to control and subdue.

Prudence symbolizes the rational and judicious approach to life, guiding Masons to act with wisdom and discretion in all circumstances, particularly in safeguarding the integrity of the Craft.

Prudence symbolizes the wise application of reason to guide one's life and actions, ensuring both present and future well-being. It represents the careful discernment required to protect the integrity and secrecy of Freemasonry.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The historical context of medieval guilds suggests that only worthy individuals could be trusted with the secrets of the order, implying a lesson for candidates about trustworthiness and the responsibility associated with shared knowledge.

The candidate is reminded that rewards, particularly knowledge of Divine Truth, must be earned through faithfulness and diligent effort, rather than being obtained through violence, deceit, or any other devious means. Furthermore, the Ruffians serve as a lesson to recognize and overcome one's own internal adversaries, such as ignorance, uncontrolled passions, and negative attitudes.

It is important for a Mason to understand the concepts of Regularity and Recognition to ensure proper interaction within the Craft and to avoid associating with Clandestine Lodges. This knowledge ensures that one's Masonic activities are conducted within legitimate and recognized jurisdictions.

A Mason must cultivate prudence as a fundamental virtue to guide their conduct, make wise decisions, and protect the sacred trust of Freemasonry by never divulging its secrets, signs, tokens, or words, especially in public or mixed company.

A Mason must cultivate prudence as a core virtue, using reason to guide all decisions and actions. This virtue is crucial for maintaining proper conduct within the Lodge and in the wider world, and especially for safeguarding the secrets of Freemasonry from unauthorized disclosure.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.
tradition_notes: The Hiramic Legend became part of Masonic Traditions between 1723 and 1738, not earlier.
There is no mention of Hiram Abiff in the Old Charges (Regius Poem, Matthew Cooke manuscript, 1632 document).
Anderson's Constitutions of 1723 make no mention of the Hiramic tragedy, but the 1738 edition includes the three ruffians killing the Prince of Architects.
The structure of Masonic degrees resembles medieval craft guild admission requirements: oath, rules, regulations, and penalties.
Masonic Law is based in part on Anderson's The Constitutions of the Free-Masons, originally published in 1723.
Anderson's Constitutions listed commonly accepted rules for Grand Lodges, Lodges, and individual members.

Examples of these rules include: acceptance of Candidates irrespective of personal religious beliefs, the Holy Bible, Square, and Compass displayed upon the Altar at all times, the acceptance of men only, and the Hiramic Legend as an integral part of the Third Degree.
The Second Section recites the historical traditions of the Institution.
The organization of Freemasonry into three Symbolic Degrees is a foundational tradition.
The use of peculiar modes of recognition and specific ceremonial forms are traditional aspects of the degrees.
The preservation of the institution's elements through secrecy is a long-standing tradition.

Intellectual knowledge not conforming to orthodox teachings could lead to accusations of heresy.
The dire consequences of the Ruffians' actions serve as a caution against attempting to obtain knowledge or rewards through illicit or unearned means.
A Clandestine Lodge is one that is not working with a legitimate charter from a Grand Lodge and is considered 'in the dark'.
Only Brethren from RECOGNIZED Masonic jurisdictions are permitted to visit our Lodges.
Masons must be particularly attentive to prudence in all strange and mixed companies.

No contention should ever exist among members of the society of friends and brothers.

entries.title: Allegory, Exoteric, and Esoteric: Definitions
entries.short_summary: This section provides definitions for the terms "Allegory," "Exoteric," and "Esoteric," sourced from the Oxford English Dictionary. Allegory is defined as the use of symbols in a story or picture to convey a hidden or ulterior meaning, typically moral or political, or the symbolic representation itself. Exoteric refers to knowledge or doctrines intended for or intelligible to the general public or outer circle of disciples. Esoteric refers to philosophical doctrines or modes of speech designed for, or intelligible only by, an inner circle of advanced or privileged disciples, implying privacy and confidentiality.

entries.title: The Exoteric Teaching: Virtue, Justice, and Merit
entries.short_summary: The exoteric teaching derived from the Hiram Abiff narrative focuses on virtue, justice, and merit. Hiram exemplifies virtue ethics, demonstrating integrity regardless of consequences, in contrast to consequentialist ethics. The concept of justice is presented as universal, emphasizing that actions have consequences. Additionally, the teaching promotes earning one's achievements and believing in one's merits, establishing these as foundational principles of the Hiram Abiff story.

Source section: Truth

Truth is presented as a divine attribute and the fundamental basis for all virtues. It is identified as the first lesson taught in Masonry, guiding members to regulate their conduct. Adherence to truth fosters sincerity, plain dealing, and mutual prosperity, eliminating hypocrisy and deceit. The text also alludes to the four Cardinal Virtues: Temperance, Fortitude, Prudence, and Justice.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Musical Selection

This section details the 'Musical Selection' portion of a Masonic gathering, which includes a Chaplain's prayer invoking divine blessing for the proceedings and for those about to assume leadership roles. Following the prayer, the Marshal delivers an address explaining the significance of observing the Festival of St. John the Evangelist. The Marshal emphasizes that Freemasonry's purpose extends beyond social enjoyment, focusing on duty, reconsecration through solemn promises, and the propagation of core principles. The discourse highlights Freemasonry's historical influence, its role in promoting liberty, equality, fraternity, law, and order, and its teachings on the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, and the importance of loving God and neighbor. It also stresses the Craft's commitment to elevating humanity, stimulating patriotism, encouraging liberal arts and sciences, promoting education, and upholding the dignity of labor, while remaining aloof from specific creeds.

The text explains the purpose of the Craft and announces the upcoming induction of chosen brethren into their respective offices to preside over the Lodge for the next twelve months.

Source section: The Worshipful Master

The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The narrative of Hiram Abiff symbolically conveys foundational principles of virtue, justice, and merit, illustrating the importance of integrity and ethical conduct in life.

The pledge symbolizes loyalty to the United States, its republican form of government, national unity under God, and the commitment to liberty and justice for all citizens.

Every figure, character, and emblem depicted in a Lodge carries a moral meaning.

Truth is symbolized as a divine attribute and the foundational principle for all virtues, guiding Masonic conduct and fostering integrity within the fraternity.

Justice is described as the "very cement and support of civil society," symbolizing its foundational and binding role in human organization and moral order.

The "cement of brotherly love and affection" symbolizes the strong bond and unity that should exist among Masons, forming a sacred society of friends and brothers. The "noble contention, or rather emulation, of who can best work and best agree" symbolizes a constructive rivalry focused on self-improvement and harmonious cooperation rather than discord.

The 'Naked Heart' symbolizes the inherent transparency of one's true self and deeds before a higher, ultimate justice. It represents the inability to hide one's inner thoughts and actions from divine or cosmic accountability, emphasizing that all will eventually be brought to light.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The Square teaches to regulate actions by rule and line, harmonizing conduct with principles of morality and virtue. The Compasses teach to limit desires, enabling one to rise to eminence by merit, live respected, and die regretted. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pressing forward in virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Holy Writings are presented as an inestimable gift from God, the Great Light in Masonry, guiding to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man.

The Wardens' role as examples of good order and regularity symbolizes the importance of leadership by virtue and adherence to Masonic laws. Their duty to diffuse light and impart knowledge represents the continuous flow of wisdom and instruction within the Craft, ensuring its perpetuation and the enlightenment of its members.

The Grand Lecturer's office symbolizes the guardianship of Masonic knowledge, the integrity of ritual, and the enduring nature of the Fraternity's foundational principles. It represents the commitment to enlighten the uninformed and ensure the continuity of ancient traditions and Landmarks, acting as a beacon for the Institution's design and tendency.

The phrase 'All stand upon the level' symbolizes the principle of equality among all Masons within the Lodge, regardless of external status or wealth.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Candidates learn to embody virtue ethics, acting with integrity irrespective of potential consequences. They are taught the universal principle of justice, understanding that actions lead to corresponding outcomes, and the importance of earning accomplishments and having faith in their own merits.

While not a direct Masonic lesson, the pledge reinforces principles of civic duty, patriotism, and adherence to the ideals of liberty and justice, which are compatible with Masonic teachings on good citizenship and moral conduct.

Freemasonry is an ancient and moral science. All symbols and elements within the Lodge are designed to teach and inculcate the practice of virtue.

The primary lesson for a candidate is to be good and true, understanding that truth is the foundation of all virtue and should govern their actions and interactions, leading to sincerity and mutual support.

A Mason must consistently practice Justice, ensuring that all individuals receive their rightful due without prejudice. This virtue is paramount for personal integrity and the stability of society, aligning with both divine and human laws.

Candidates are taught the importance of historical tradition, the virtues of fortitude and integrity, and the paramount value of brotherly love and affection. They learn that true Masonic unity is fostered by a spirit of noble emulation, where members strive to excel in their duties and maintain harmony, rather than engaging in destructive contention.

Candidates are taught that they are ultimately accountable for all their thoughts, words, and actions, regardless of whether these are known to other men. This lesson encourages integrity, moral conduct, and the understanding that justice will prevail.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

The candidate learns that leadership within Masonry requires a profound understanding of its principles, exemplary conduct, and a commitment to teaching and guiding others. It underscores the importance of self-improvement, learning from others' strengths and weaknesses, and being prepared to assume greater responsibilities for the benefit of the fraternity.

While primarily a charge to an officer, the text implicitly teaches the importance of upholding Masonic duties and obligations, the value of continuous learning and instruction, and the necessity of preserving the Fraternity's ancient ritual and Landmarks. It also highlights the expectation for officers to exemplify promptness and integrity in their conduct.

Candidates learn that Freemasonry is a universal brotherhood, transcending specific religions or political affiliations. They are taught the importance of obedience to civil law, the fundamental equality of all members, and the principle of brotherhood towards all people. The lesson emphasizes that true merit lies in one's actions and ability to foster harmony.

entries.title: Mackey's Seventeen Meanings of the Raising
entries.short_summary: This section introduces Albert Mackey's list of seventeen different symbolic meanings or interpretations for the raising of Hiram Abiff. It highlights the diversity of perspectives on this central Masonic event, ranging from historical death to various allegories, religious parallels, and even political or philosophical interpretations. The text also notes that three specific meanings—the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend—will be examined in more detail.
entries.full_summary:
This section introduces Albert Mackey's list of seventeen different symbolic meanings or interpretations for the raising of Hiram Abiff. It highlights the diversity of perspectives on this central Masonic event, ranging from historical death to various allegories, religious parallels, and even political or philosophical interpretations. The text also notes that three specific meanings—the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend—will be examined in more detail.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0013 -->
### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: Raising of a Candidate

This section clarifies the meaning of being 'raised to the Sublime Degree of Master Mason,' explaining that 'sublime' signifies 'not to be excelled' and positions the Master Mason Degree as the pinnacle of Symbolic Freemasonry. It traces the term 'raised' to operative masonry, where only a few journeymen were elevated to Master Craftsman. Historically, only the presiding officer was a Master Mason, but the fraternity evolved to divide initiation into three degrees: Entered Apprentice, Fellow of the Craft, and Master Mason. Ultimately, being raised means a new member has achieved full membership in a Symbolic Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:basic-masonic-education-course:section-0013 -->
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The raising of Hiram Abiff is presented as having at least seventeen different symbolic meanings, according to Albert Mackey. These interpretations include: the actual death of Hiram Abiff, the legend of Osiris, an allegory of the setting sun, the expulsion of Adam from Eden, the death of Abel, Noah's entry into the ark, Joseph's mourning for Jacob, an astronomical problem, the death and resurrection of Christ, the persecution of the Templars, the violent death of Charles I, a drama invented by Cromwell, a representation of old age, a drama of regeneration, a savage ceremony of initiation, a memorial to the murder of Thomas Becket, and an invention by the Jacobites. These diverse meanings underscore the rich allegorical potential of the Hiramic legend.

The term 'sublime' in 'Sublime Degree of Master Mason' symbolizes that this degree is the highest and 'not to be excelled' within Symbolic Freemasonry. The term 'raised' symbolically connects to the operative tradition where stonemasons were 'elevated' to Master Craftsman status, signifying a significant advancement and attainment of full membership.
entries.candidate_lesson:
A candidate should understand that the central event of the raising of Hiram Abiff is open to a multitude of interpretations, reflecting its profound allegorical depth. This encourages a broader perspective on Masonic symbolism and the various philosophical, historical, and religious lenses through which the legend can be understood, emphasizing that its meaning is not singular but multifaceted.

The candidate learns that attaining the Master Mason Degree signifies reaching the pinnacle of Symbolic Freemasonry and achieving full membership within a Symbolic Lodge. This elevation carries historical significance rooted in operative masonry's structure of advancement.
tradition_notes: Albert Mackey compiled a list of at least seventeen different traditional or proposed meanings for the raising of Hiram Abiff, indicating a long history of diverse interpretations within Masonic thought.
The text specifically mentions the Christ allegory, the Rosicrucian Temple Legend, and the Osiris legend as significant interpretations deserving closer examination.
The term 'raised' originates from operative Masonry, where journeymen were elevated to Master Craftsman.
Historically, only the presiding officer of a Lodge held the status of a Master Mason.
The fraternity's system of initiation evolved to be divided into three parts: Entered Apprentice, Fellow of the Craft, and Master Masons.

entries.title: The Christ Allegory
entries.short_summary: This section explores the "Christ Allegory" in relation to the raising of Hiram Abiff, presenting Mackey's view that the Masonic text does not support a literal resurrection drama, but rather a substitution. It notes common parallels drawn between Hiram's raising and Christ's resurrection, and between Hiram's three murderers and historical figures like Caesar's agent, the Sanhedrin, and an incited mob. However, it also cautions against the likelihood of early Masons creating such a ceremony directly over something considered so sacred.
entries.full_summary:
This section explores the "Christ Allegory" in relation to the raising of Hiram Abiff, presenting Mackey's view that the Masonic text does not support a literal resurrection drama, but rather a substitution. It notes common parallels drawn between Hiram's raising and Christ's resurrection, and between Hiram's three murderers and historical figures like Caesar's agent, the Sanhedrin, and an incited mob. However, it also cautions against the likelihood of early Masons creating such a ceremony directly over something considered so sacred.

### Enrichment from Basic Masonic Education Course
Source section: The Lion of the Tribe of Judah

This section explores the symbolism of the lion, particularly as the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah'. It details its historical association with might, royalty, the Hebrew Tribe of Judah, and King Solomon. The text also delves into its medieval symbolism of resurrection, referencing tales of lion cubs being brought to life by their father's breath and roaring lions representing the resurrection on the Last Day. The lion's majestic nature and association with the sun are also noted.

### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Ii. Essentials in Belief

This section outlines the four essential beliefs required in Freemasonry: belief in the existence of God, the immortality of the soul, a resurrection to future life, and the authority of a book of revealed Divine Law.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0165 -->
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The raising of Hiram Abiff from his earthly sepulcher is seen by some as analogous to Christ's resurrection. The three murderers are symbolically linked to Caesar's agent (representing the state), the Sanhedrin (representing the church), and an incited mob, drawing parallels to the forces involved in Christ's crucifixion.

The lion symbolizes might, royalty, and the Tribe of Judah. It is a title for King Solomon. In the Middle Ages, it symbolized resurrection, specifically through tales of lion cubs being revived by their father's breath, and roaring lions representing the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day. The lion is also considered the 'king' of beasts and is associated with the sun due to its mane.

These beliefs serve as foundational tenets, representing the spiritual and moral framework upon which Masonic philosophy and practice are built, rather than individual symbols to be interpreted.
entries.candidate_lesson:
The text primarily discusses different interpretations of the Hiramic legend, particularly the debate around whether Hiram's 'raising' constitutes a resurrection allegory or a substitution. It encourages critical thinking about the symbolic meanings within the ritual.

Candidates are taught that these four fundamental beliefs are essential prerequisites for Masonic membership, forming the spiritual and moral foundation of the Craft and guiding their personal development.
tradition_notes: Mackey's dictionary is cited as a source for interpreting the Hiramic legend, specifically regarding the nature of Hiram's 'raising.' The text also touches upon the historical context of early Masons and their likely approach to sacred allegories.
The lion was the sign of the Tribe of Judah, the royal tribe of the Hebrew Nation. All Kings of Judah were called the 'Lion of the Tribe of Judah', including King Solomon.
In the Middle Ages, there were common tales that a lion cub lay dead for three days until its father's breath brought it back to life, symbolizing resurrection.
Representations of roaring lions symbolized the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day.
These four points represent fundamental and universal tenets of Freemasonry, forming the basis of a candidate's spiritual and moral commitment.
caution_notes: Mackey's dictionary argues that the Masonic text does not support a drama of resurrection for Hiram Abiff, but rather a substitution of one man for another. It is also noted as unlikely that early Masons would have created a ceremony directly over something as sacred as the Christ allegory.

entries.title: The Rosicrucian Temple Legend
entries.short_summary: Rudolf Steiner, a modern Rosicrucian, presented a unique Temple Legend in 1904, distinct from the Christian allegory of Hiram Abiff. This legend describes two lineages: Cain, a direct descendant of the Elohim, whose line developed art and science, and Abel, a descendant of Adam and Eve, whose line (Seth) possessed divine wisdom. Hiram Abiff, from Cain's lineage, was a master architect called by Solomon (from Seth's line) to build the Temple. Jealousy arose between them over the Queen of Sheba. When Hiram's masterpiece, the Molten Sea, was sabotaged by three apprentices, Hiram cast himself into the fire, was initiated into the Mystery of Fire, received a hammer and Golden Triangle, and completed the casting. The Queen of Sheba then chose Hiram. He was subsequently murdered by the apprentices, but before dying, he threw the Golden Triangle into a well, prophesying a son who would complete his work. The Golden Triangle and Molten Sea were preserved together in the Holy of Holies.
entries.full_summary:

Hiram Abiff, from Cain's lineage, was a master architect called by Solomon (from Seth's line) to build the Temple. Jealousy arose between them over the Queen of Sheba. When Hiram's masterpiece, the Molten Sea, was sabotaged by three apprentices, Hiram cast himself into the fire, was initiated into the Mystery of Fire, received a hammer and Golden Triangle, and completed the casting. The Queen of Sheba then chose Hiram. He was subsequently murdered by the apprentices, but before dying, he threw the Golden Triangle into a well, prophesying a son who would complete his work. The Golden Triangle and Molten Sea were preserved together in the Holy of Holies.

entries.symbolic_meaning:
The legend symbolizes the necessary union between two 'races' of mankind: those like Solomon, possessing divine wisdom (Sons of Seth, religious, calm, objective, but lacking enthusiasm), and those like Hiram, conversant with the 'fire of wishes and desires' (Sons of Cain, impulsive, creators of arts and sciences). Hiram Abiff's ability to combine the 'water of calm wisdom' with the 'fire of passion and desire' is interpreted as the creation of enduring brotherhood.

The lion symbolizes might, royalty, and the Tribe of Judah. It is a title for King Solomon. In the Middle Ages, it symbolized resurrection, specifically through tales of lion cubs being revived by their father's breath, and roaring lions representing the resurrection of the dead on the Last Day. The lion is also considered the 'king' of beasts and is associated with the sun due to its mane.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate learns the importance of uniting divine wisdom with human passion, creativity, and practical skill to achieve lasting and durable brotherhood. It emphasizes that both intellectual understanding and the drive for artistic and scientific creation are essential for the completion of great work and the establishment of enduring human connections.
tradition_notes: This Rosicrucian Temple Legend was presented by Rudolf Steiner in 1904.
It offers distinctly different ideas compared to the Christian allegory of Hiram Abiff.
Rudolf Steiner founded a Rosicrucian school of Anthroposophy.

entries.title: Isis and Osiris
entries.short_summary: This section explores the theory that the legend of Hiram Abiff is a Masonic interpretation of the ancient Egyptian myth of Osiris. It provides a synopsis of the Osiris myth, detailing how Osiris was tricked and sealed in a chest by his evil brother Seth, floated to Byblos, and later dismembered into fourteen parts. His sister-wife Isis, along with Nephthys and Thoth, attempted to reassemble and raise him, with Thoth succeeding using a special grip. The text highlights numerous parallels between the Osiris myth and Freemasonry, including shared symbols like the ladder, pillar, acacia, and casket, as well as narrative similarities such as betrayal, murder by a brother, hasty burial, and the concept of a lost word or kingdom.
entries.full_summary:
This section explores the theory that the legend of Hiram Abiff is a Masonic interpretation of the ancient Egyptian myth of Osiris. It provides a synopsis of the Osiris myth, detailing how Osiris was tricked and sealed in a chest by his evil brother Seth, floated to Byblos, and later dismembered into fourteen parts. His sister-wife Isis, along with Nephthys and Thoth, attempted to reassemble and raise him, with Thoth succeeding using a special grip. The text highlights numerous parallels between the Osiris myth and Freemasonry, including shared symbols like the ladder, pillar, acacia, and casket, as well as narrative similarities such as betrayal, murder by a brother, hasty burial, and the concept of a lost word or kingdom.
